title: Poema Morale (Digby 4 version)
summary: CME00086 is the repository copy of the complete University of Michigan electronic transcription of Julius Zupitza's 1878 article 'Zum Poema Morale,' Anglia 1: 5–38. The article prints the Digby 4 copy continuously on pp. 6–32, compares it with the five then-known other copy streams, proposes a stemma, and argues for composition around 1170. Zupitza says that his text follows Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Digby 4, ff. 97r–110v, apart from explicitly listed normalization and correction practices. His ten stanzas supplied from Trinity witness T occur only inside notes at eight Digby lacunae; they do not turn the main stream into an eclectic Trinity text. The comparison to E/e/J/L/T is routed through the earlier printed editions Zupitza names because direct consultation of those physical codices is not established. The exact 1878 volume and all named earlier editions are cached complete. No item-specific public Digby 4 facsimile was verified, so the record retains the official catalog and Bodleian whole-object imaging route. Complete public facsimiles of Jesus 29 and Trinity B.14.52 are cached as hard-linked whole-codex bundles with the Poema Morale portions mapped inside them; Egerton 613 has a complete 197-canvas viewer but is not bulk-cached under the British Library's geographically qualified terms, and Lambeth 487's legacy viewer remains access-gated. The XML preserves the complete printed page run but replaces Zupitza's graphical stemma with only a FIGDESC, losing the diagram's geometry.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00086
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO and VID all identify CME00086; the file is 80,809 bytes.
TEMPHEAD records a structure and proof review dated 2016-09-15, not the original encoding date.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00086
entities.description: A public electronic transcription of Zupitza's complete article on printed pp. 5–38, including German introduction and studies, Digby text, apparatus, page and folio milestones, and a textual description but not an image of the printed stemma.

entities.label: Julius Zupitza, Zum Poema Morale (1878)
entities.description: Zupitza's complete article introduces six witnesses, prints the Digby 4 version, compares their readings and order, constructs a stemma, rejects a proposed tenth- or eleventh-century exemplar, and dates the work about 1170.

bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Zum Poema Morale,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 1 (1878): 5–38.
bibliographic.title: Zum Poema Morale

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Anglia volume 1 (1878)
entities.description: Complete 622-page scan of the carrier volume; Zupitza's article is printed pp. 5–38 / PDF pages 17–50 / Internet Archive leaves 17–50.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Digby 4
entities.description: The principal and directly transcribed witness. Zupitza places Poema Morale on the first fourteen leaves of a sixteen-leaf quire, ff. 97r–110v, in one ruled column of twenty-eight short lines per page; he assigns its hand to the early thirteenth century and its language to Kent. The current Bodleian catalog describes the whole codex as a thirteenth-century manuscript of 112 leaves.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Thirteenth century; Zupitza more specifically proposed the beginning of the century

notes: Poema Morale occupies ff. 97r–110v; the entire codex, not only these leaves, is the desired facsimile object.

entities.label: George Hickes, Linguarum veterum septentrionalium thesaurus, volume 1 (1705)
entities.description: The earlier printed source known to Zupitza for thirty-seven selected Digby stanzas, beginning at the grammar's p. 222; it is an excerpt, not a complete Digby transcription.

bibliographic.citation: George Hickes, Linguarum veterum septentrionalium thesaurus grammatico-criticus et archaeologicus, vol. 1 (Oxford, 1705), Grammatica Anglo-Saxonica, p. 222 ff.
bibliographic.title: Linguarum veterum septentrionalium thesaurus grammatico-criticus et archaeologicus

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Hickes's 1705 Thesaurus volume 1
entities.description: Complete 796-page scan of the printed volume containing the selected Digby extract at Grammatica Anglo-Saxonica p. 222 ff.

entities.description: The edition Zupitza cites for Trinity witness T on pp. 220–232; Zupitza uses T both for continuous comparison and for ten stanzas printed in notes at eight Digby lacunae.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Morris's Old English Homilies, Second Series
entities.description: Complete 319-page scan; A Moral Ode occupies printed pp. 220–232 / PDF pages 259–271.

entities.label: London, British Library Egerton MS 613
entities.description: A composite parchment miscellany containing two Poema Morale copies: E at ff. 7r–12v and the later-hand e at ff. 64r–70v. The first belongs to a thirteenth-century section copied by scribe 2; the second belongs to a first-half-of-the-thirteenth-century section copied by scribe 1 and lacks the final twenty-six lines.

physical_description.extent: 74 folios plus ten modern paper endleaves at each end
notes: The public digital object contains 197 canvases from front cover through spine.

entities.label: British Library complete IIIF object for Egerton MS 613
entities.description: The complete 197-canvas public viewer inventory, with E mapped to canvases 39–50 and e mapped to canvases 153–166. It is viewable but was not bulk-cached because the manifest's public-domain statement excludes the UK and the linked BL terms require caution about systematic extraction.

notes: Sequence begins with front cover and ends with spine.

entities.label: Jesus College, Oxford MS 29
entities.description: The Jesus witness occupies current ff. 169r–174v / Zupitza's legacy ff. 242r–247v. The complete digital object proves a consistent seventy-three-folio offset between these numbering systems.

date_statements.label: Poema Morale copy
date_statements.value: Around the middle of the thirteenth century

physical_description.extent: 257 folios in the complete digital object, with boards and flyleaves

entities.label: Complete Digital Bodleian object for Jesus College MS 29
entities.description: Complete 532-canvas color facsimile from upper board through tail. The Poema Morale copy occupies current ff. 169r–174v / canvases 345–356.

notes: Boards and insides, four flyleaves, spine, fore-edge, head, tail, all ff. 1r–257v and adjacent contents are present; no canvas explicitly labels pastedowns.

entities.reproduction_format: Full-color IIIF JPEG page images with color target and scale on sampled manuscript leaves

entities.label: London, Lambeth Palace Library MS 487
entities.description: A small West Midlands manuscript copied around the turn of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries. The Poema Morale copy is written as prose at ff. 59v–65r and ends after the equivalent of Digby stanza 130 / line 270.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Late twelfth or early thirteenth century

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.52
entities.description: The close comparison witness used through Morris's edition. The current Trinity catalog and DIMEV locate Poema Morale at ff. 2r–9v; Zupitza prints ten T stanzas inside notes at eight lacunae in D.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Late twelfth or early thirteenth century

physical_description.extent: 91 folios; 216 published canvases including covers, flyleaves, stubs and UV alternates

entities.label: Complete published Trinity IIIF inventory for MS B.14.52
entities.description: Complete 216-canvas published inventory, cached at a practical access resolution and mapped to Poema Morale at ff. 2r–9v / canvases 13–28.

notes: Canvas 1 is the front cover; the ordinary sequence ends at canvas 206 with the back cover; canvases 207–216 are UV alternates. No spine or edge views are published.

entities.reproduction_format: Full-color IIIF JPEG page images and UV alternates

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse version 1.0
entities.description: Versioned CC BY 4.0 data identifying Poema Morale as DIMEV/NIMEV 1272, giving seven complete witness entries, their loci and extents, and listing Zupitza 1878 for Digby witness D.

entities.label: Betty Hill, The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life (1977)
entities.description: A modern study of the title, seven complete witnesses, publication history, textual relationship, date, localization, metre and homiletic content; it cautions that each witness represents active textual reshaping.

bibliographic.citation: Betty Hill, 'The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life, Formerly the Poema Morale or A Moral Ode,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 9 (1977): 97–144.

scope.notes: The textual content is complete, but the printed stemma's geometry is not reproduced: the XML supplies only FIGDESC 'family tree of manuscripts'.

subject.description: Section I, Das Poema Morale nach D, main text
locators: 191 numbered main LGs / 764 short-line L elements

scope.notes: Zupitza states that his print follows D exactly apart from listed expansion, capitalization, word-division, punctuation and correction practices. Ten supplied T stanzas remain passage-scoped notes and are excluded from this main-stream relation.
Zupitza lists the exact Digby stanza numbers previously printed by Hickes; Hickes is a prior excerpt, not the source of Zupitza's continuous transcription.

notes: This does not determine rights in modern photographs, interfaces, editions or encodings.

notes: Public access is not permission to redistribute the PDF.

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded letters are printed in italics.
editorial_practices.description: Zupitza explicitly says he expands abbreviations and marks the expansions with italic type.

editorial_practices.representation: Proper names receive capitals; selected words such as god, riche, mai and muchel are lowercased at cited locations.
editorial_practices.description: The article enumerates its capitalization changes rather than reproducing witness case mechanically.

editorial_practices.representation: Word division is regulated and editorial punctuation supplied; manuscript points are omitted.
editorial_practices.description: Zupitza says the witness often points stanza ends, sometimes short-line ends and rarely within the verse, but his edition does not retain those points.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Unclear Digby readings and eight lacuna loci
editorial_practices.representation: Other witnesses are quoted in notes; ten omitted stanzas are supplied from T inside nested note groups.
editorial_practices.description: The continuous main stream remains D. The ten T stanzas appear after D stanzas 14, 21, 44 (two), 128 (two), 160, 167, 173 and 175 and are encoded as forty note-descendant L elements.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Relationships among D, T, E, e, J and L
editorial_practices.representation: A graphical stemma proposes U dividing into Y and Z, with further X and W groupings.
editorial_practices.description: The stemma is Zupitza's 1878 reconstruction, not a current consensus and not evidence that any surviving witness is the lost archetype U.

editorial_practices.representation: Thirty-four PB elements preserve the complete consecutive page range.
editorial_practices.description: Unlike many selected CME article encodings, CME00086 retains the whole article rather than only the edited verse.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The article's italic German prose, witness labels and abbreviation expansions
editorial_practices.description: The 835 italic spans include both broad editorial italics and semantically significant expanded letters. Context, not the element alone, determines whether italics mark expansion.

editorial_practices.representation: Empty FIGURE with FIGDESC 'family tree of manuscripts'; no image, branches or node geometry.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding signals that a figure exists but cannot reconstruct the tree from its markup. The cached full-volume scan is required to review the actual diagram.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The 1878 print labels the stanza after 155 as 165 and later labels the real 165 again.
editorial_practices.representation: The XML faithfully repeats LG/@N and HEAD 165 at the first occurrence.
editorial_practices.description: This is a print-source numbering error for stanza 156, not an independent XML corruption; the sequence resumes 157.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed p. 27 / XML LG after N=155

evidence.citation: Structural and content audit of pinned CME00086.xml against the complete Anglia scan.
evidence.locator: Counts and hierarchy under BODY/DIV1; printed pp. 5, 27, 32, 36 and 38
evidence.summary: Counts one DIV1, four DIV2, 201 LG, 808 L, 79 NOTE, 34 PB, 28 MILESTONE and 835 italic HI. Section I has 191 direct LG / 764 direct L; ten nested T-supply LG / forty L; Section II has four quoted L. Confirms the complete p. 5–38 run, print-source 165-for-156 typo, and loss of stemma geometry in FIGDESC-only markup.

evidence.summary: Modern scholarly evidence for title history, seven complete versions, late-twelfth-century composition, tentative South-East Midlands localization, metre, genre, content and revisions to older stemmatic assumptions.

evidence.citation: Local checksum, size, PDF, ZIP-inventory, image-profile and hard-link audit of CME00086 source-cache deliverables.
evidence.locator: all manifest-referenced files; Jesus canvases 345–356; Trinity canvases 13–28; first and last published surfaces
evidence.summary: Verifies every local copy's SHA-256 and byte count; complete carrier-volume boundaries; complete Jesus and Trinity source inventories; exact target folio/canvas mappings; sampled image dimensions and color; and shared-cache inode identity.

open_questions.question: Does the Bodleian already hold or permit creation of a complete color digital facsimile of MS. Digby 4, and what exact private-storage, research, Git-publication, print-on-demand and commercial permissions would govern it?
next_steps: Email imaging@bodleian.ox.ac.uk with the exact shelfmark and request the complete current physical object: boards/covers and insides, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf and edges where offered.
Map Poema Morale separately to ff. 97r–110v and request an image inventory/canvas concordance.
Request separate written terms for private cache retention, public Git distribution, noncommercial publication, commercial print-on-demand and sublicensing.

open_questions.question: Did Zupitza inspect E, e, J, L or T directly, or did he construct all non-D comparisons solely from the cited Furnivall and Morris editions?
next_steps: Consult Zupitza's correspondence, working papers and later methodological statements.
Until direct physical use is evidenced, retain the printed editions as the immediate comparison layer and the manuscripts as their sources.

open_questions.question: Which parts of Zupitza's 1878 U–Y/Z–X/W stemma remain accepted after Hill's revised analysis and later witness scholarship?
next_steps: Compare Hill's full textual arguments and later diplomatic editions witness by witness.
Treat Zupitza's U, Y, Z, X and W as historical editorial hypotheses, not material witnesses.

open_questions.question: What do the two legacy Lambeth LUNA items for MS 487 contain, and is a complete whole-codex surrogate available after registration or by request?
next_steps: Register through the official access route or ask Lambeth Palace Library for an inventory.
Request all physical surfaces, not only ff. 59v–65r, and keep the work mapping separate.

open_questions.question: What permissions govern UK bulk retention and republication of the complete Egerton 613 IIIF image set?
next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile the manifest's 'Public Domain in most countries, other than the UK' wording with current systematic-extraction and publication terms.
Do not bulk-cache or redistribute the sequence until that scope is clear.

open_questions.question: Should downstream display silently correct the 1878 print's first stanza number 165 to 156, or preserve and annotate the source error?
next_steps: Preserve the source value in diplomatic output.
Add a non-destructive display annotation or normalization only if the publication workflow can distinguish source and corrected numbering.

review_notes: The medieval work, each manuscript witness, each nineteenth-century printed edition, the U-M encoding and the repository artifact are distinct layers.
Zupitza's main text is a Digby transcription. Ten T stanzas are passage-scoped note supplies at eight Digby lacunae, not a second continuous base.
Current Jesus 29 foliation is seventy-three lower than Zupitza's legacy foliation: current 169r–174v equals legacy 242r–247v.
The Trinity current catalog locates the poem at ff. 2r–9v / canvases 13–28. Hall's older 1r–8v numbering is not substituted here.
A complete public viewer is not automatically a downloadable or redistributable corpus asset. Egerton 613 remains linked but uncached under the conservative terms reading.
The cached Jesus and Trinity files cover the full provider-published source objects, while work_portion maps only the Poema Morale leaves. Completeness and target scope are intentionally separate.
No target-only manuscript excerpt is treated as a complete codex. Digby and Lambeth retain mediated whole-object request routes.
The complete Anglia scan is necessary to recover the actual stemma because the XML retains only a generic figure description.
Local caching and hard-link reuse do not grant permission to publish the files in Git or use noncommercially licensed images in commercial print-on-demand outputs.
